questions.stem: Which cloud object storage service does Snowflake use as its storage layer when deployed on AWS?

options.text: Amazon EBS (Elastic Block Store)
Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service)
Amazon EFS (Elastic File System)
Amazon Glacier

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake uses Amazon S3 as its underlying object storage on AWS. Data is stored as compressed, columnar micro-partitions in S3. A is wrong because EBS is block storage attached to EC2 instances and is not used as Snowflake's data layer. C is wrong because EFS is a file system service not used by Snowflake's storage layer. D is wrong because Glacier is archival storage and not the active storage layer used by Snowflake.
questions.reference: Snowflake Architecture — Storage Layer on AWS

questions.stem: A Snowflake virtual warehouse is set to auto-suspend after 5 minutes of inactivity and auto-resume is enabled. A query is submitted when the warehouse is suspended. What happens?

options.text: The query fails with an error because the warehouse is suspended.
The query is queued for up to 24 hours until the warehouse is manually resumed.
The warehouse automatically resumes, the query executes, and credits are consumed.
The query executes against the Cloud Services layer without consuming warehouse credits.

questions.explanation: C is correct because with auto-resume enabled, Snowflake automatically starts the warehouse when a query is submitted, the query executes, and credits are charged for the warehouse uptime. A is wrong because auto-resume prevents query failures from a suspended state. B is wrong because there is no 24-hour queuing behavior for suspended warehouses — auto-resume handles this transparently. D is wrong because most queries require the virtual warehouse compute layer, not just the Cloud Services layer.
questions.reference: Snowflake Architecture — Auto-Suspend and Auto-Resume

questions.stem: Which statement CORRECTLY describes how Snowflake's compute and storage layers interact?

options.text: Each virtual warehouse has dedicated storage that it owns and manages.
Multiple virtual warehouses can read from and write to the same storage layer concurrently without contention.
Only one virtual warehouse can access the storage layer at a time to prevent data corruption.
The storage layer is co-located with the compute layer to minimize network latency.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake's architecture separates storage and compute, allowing multiple virtual warehouses to concurrently access the same shared storage layer. This is a core architectural advantage — different workloads (ETL, reporting, ad-hoc) can run on separate warehouses without contention. A is wrong because warehouses do not own dedicated storage; they all share the centralized storage layer. C is wrong because Snowflake explicitly allows concurrent multi-warehouse access to storage. D is wrong because compute and storage are separate — compute nodes read from object storage (S3/Azure/GCS) over the network.
questions.reference: Snowflake Architecture — Separation of Compute and Storage

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to track and limit the number of compute credits used by a specific virtual warehouse. Which Snowflake object should they create?

options.text: Network policy
Resource monitor
Row access policy
Task

questions.explanation: B is correct because a resource monitor allows administrators to define credit quotas and trigger actions (notify, suspend, suspend immediately) when a warehouse reaches defined thresholds. It can be applied at the account or individual warehouse level. A is wrong because network policies control IP-based access, not credit consumption. C is wrong because row access policies control data visibility in tables, not compute resource usage. D is wrong because tasks are used to schedule SQL statements, not to limit warehouse credit consumption.
questions.reference: Snowflake Architecture — Resource Monitors

questions.stem: Which TWO of the following are characteristics of Snowflake micro-partitions? (Select TWO)

options.text: Micro-partitions are mutable and are updated in-place when rows change.
Micro-partitions are immutable; updates result in new micro-partitions being created.
Data within a micro-partition is stored in columnar format.
Each micro-partition holds exactly 1,000,000 rows.

questions.explanation: B and C are correct. Micro-partitions are immutable — when data is updated or deleted, Snowflake marks old micro-partitions as deleted and creates new ones, enabling Time Travel. Data within each micro-partition is stored in columnar format, enabling efficient column pruning and compression. A is wrong because micro-partitions are explicitly immutable. D is wrong because micro-partitions do not have a fixed row count — they are sized by compressed data volume (50–500 MB), so the row count varies by data type and compression ratio.
questions.reference: Snowflake Architecture — Micro-partition Properties

questions.stem: A company is currently on the Enterprise edition and wants to enable cross-region database replication for disaster recovery. Which statement is TRUE?

options.text: Database replication is available on all Snowflake editions including Standard.
Cross-region replication requires an upgrade to Business Critical or higher.
Database replication is available on Enterprise edition and above.
Replication is handled automatically by Snowflake for all accounts and cannot be configured.

questions.explanation: C is correct because database replication (including cross-region and cross-cloud replication) is available starting with the Enterprise edition. The company on Enterprise edition can enable replication without upgrading. A is wrong because Standard edition does not include database replication. B is wrong because upgrading to Business Critical is not required — Enterprise already supports it. D is wrong because replication must be explicitly configured by the account administrator; it is not automatic.
questions.reference: Snowflake Architecture — Database Replication, Enterprise Edition

questions.stem: A query result cache hit requires which of the following conditions to ALL be true?

options.text: The same SQL text, the same user (regardless of role), and no micro-partition changes to base tables.
The same SQL text, the same role, the same warehouse, and no data changes in the base tables within 24 hours.
The same SQL text, the same role, no data changes in the base tables, and the result was cached within the last 24 hours.
The same warehouse and the same SQL text within the same session.

questions.explanation: C is correct because the query result cache returns cached results when the exact same SQL text is submitted by a session using the same role, the underlying data has not changed, and the cached result is still within the 24-hour TTL. Notably, the same warehouse is NOT required — the result cache is in the Cloud Services layer and is warehouse-independent. A is wrong because the same user is not sufficient — it must be the same role. B is wrong because the same warehouse is not a requirement for result cache hits. D is wrong because result cache hits work across sessions, not just within the same session, and warehouse identity is irrelevant.
questions.reference: Snowflake Architecture — Query Result Cache

questions.stem: An X-Large virtual warehouse (16 credits/hr) runs for exactly 45 minutes processing an ETL job. How many credits are consumed?

options.text: 16 credits
8 credits
12 credits
32 credits

questions.explanation: C is correct because credits are billed per second (with a 60-second minimum). An X-Large warehouse costs 16 credits/hr. Running for 45 minutes = 45/60 = 0.75 hours × 16 credits/hr = 12 credits. A is wrong because 16 credits would be for a full hour. B is wrong because 8 credits would be for 30 minutes. D is wrong because 32 credits would be for a 2XL warehouse running for a full hour.
questions.reference: Snowflake Architecture — Virtual Warehouse Credit Billing

questions.stem: Which system-defined role should a Snowflake administrator use to create databases, schemas, and warehouses?

options.text: ACCOUNTADMIN
SECURITYADMIN
SYSADMIN
PUBLIC

questions.explanation: C is correct because SYSADMIN is the recommended role for creating and managing database objects such as databases, schemas, tables, and warehouses. Using SYSADMIN (rather than ACCOUNTADMIN) follows the principle of least privilege. A is wrong because while ACCOUNTADMIN can create these objects, Snowflake best practice recommends using SYSADMIN for object management and reserving ACCOUNTADMIN for account-level administration. B is wrong because SECURITYADMIN manages users and roles, not database objects. D is wrong because PUBLIC has minimal privileges and cannot create databases or warehouses.
questions.reference: Snowflake Security — RBAC Best Practices, SYSADMIN Role

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator executes: `ALTER USER analyst1 SET NETWORK_POLICY = 'corp_policy';`. What is the effect?

options.text: The network policy applies to all users in the account.
The network policy overrides the account-level network policy for analyst1 only.
The command fails because network policies can only be set at the account level.
The analyst1 user is removed from the account-level network policy and has no restrictions.

questions.explanation: B is correct because a user-level network policy overrides the account-level network policy for that specific user. This allows more granular control — for example, allowing a service account to connect from a different IP range than the corporate policy allows. A is wrong because setting a policy on a specific user does not affect all users. C is wrong because network policies CAN be set at both the account level and the individual user level. D is wrong because the user's network policy still applies the IP restrictions defined in 'corp_policy' — they are not unrestricted.
questions.reference: Snowflake Security — Network Policy User-Level Override

questions.stem: A company uses Snowflake Secure Views to share data with external partners. Why is a Secure View required instead of a regular view for data sharing?

options.text: Secure views are the only view type that can be added to a Snowflake SHARE object.
Secure views encrypt data at rest, which is required for cross-account sharing.
Secure views prevent the consumer from seeing the view definition, protecting proprietary business logic.
Secure views bypass the query result cache to ensure consumers always see fresh data.

questions.explanation: C is correct because secure views hide the underlying view definition (the DDL) from users who are not the view owner. In a data sharing context, this protects the provider's proprietary business logic and filtering conditions from being inspected by consumers. A is wrong because while secure views are required for sharing, the reason is the privacy of the definition, not the exclusivity — it is a policy requirement. B is wrong because encryption at rest is handled by Snowflake's storage layer, not by the secure view feature. D is wrong because secure views do interact with the query result cache; bypassing the cache is a different concern.
questions.reference: Snowflake Security and Data Sharing — Secure Views

questions.stem: A row access policy is created with the following function body: `RETURN CURRENT_ROLE() = 'DATA_OWNER' OR t.region = CURRENT_USER()`. This policy is applied to the `sales` table. What does this policy do?

options.text: Users with the DATA_OWNER role see all rows; other users see only rows where the region column matches their username.
Only the DATA_OWNER role can access the table; all other users are denied.
Users see all rows if their username matches the region column; DATA_OWNER has no special access.
The policy returns an error because CURRENT_USER() cannot be compared to a table column.

questions.explanation: A is correct because the policy returns TRUE (allowing the row) if either the current role is DATA_OWNER (which grants access to all rows) OR if the region column value matches the current username (which restricts other users to only their region's rows). B is wrong because the OR condition means non-DATA_OWNER users can still see rows where their username matches the region. C is wrong because DATA_OWNER sees ALL rows due to the first condition in the OR. D is wrong because CURRENT_USER() returning a string is a valid comparison to a string column.
questions.reference: Snowflake Security — Row Access Policies

questions.stem: Which TWO authentication methods does Snowflake support for service accounts used in automated pipelines? (Select TWO)

options.text: Username and password with MFA bypass
Key-pair authentication using RSA private/public keys
OAuth access tokens (e.g., Snowflake OAuth or external OAuth)
Biometric fingerprint authentication

questions.explanation: B and C are correct. Key-pair authentication is the recommended method for service accounts — the private key is stored securely on the client, and the public key is registered in Snowflake. OAuth (including Snowflake OAuth and external OAuth with providers like Okta) is also supported for programmatic access. A is wrong because password + MFA bypass is not a supported or recommended pattern for service accounts; MFA is a security control that should not be bypassed. D is wrong because biometric authentication is not a Snowflake authentication method.
questions.reference: Snowflake Security — Key-Pair Authentication, OAuth

questions.stem: A masking policy is applied to the `email` column in the `customers` table. Users with the ANALYST role see `****@****.com` while users with the MARKETING role see the full email address. Which SQL snippet CORRECTLY creates this behavior?

options.text: `CASE WHEN CURRENT_ROLE() IN ('MARKETING') THEN val ELSE '****@****.com' END`
`CASE WHEN IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION('ANALYST') THEN val ELSE '****@****.com' END`
`CASE WHEN CURRENT_USER() = 'MARKETING' THEN val ELSE '****@****.com' END`
`IF(CURRENT_ROLE() = 'ANALYST', val, '****@****.com')`

questions.explanation: A is correct because masking policy bodies use `CURRENT_ROLE()` to determine the active role and return the unmasked value for privileged roles (MARKETING) or the masked value otherwise. B is wrong because IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION checks whether the ANALYST role is in session — showing the full value to ANALYST is the inverse of the requirement. C is wrong because CURRENT_USER() returns the username, not the role, so comparing it to 'MARKETING' would not correctly match the role. D is wrong because IF() is not standard Snowflake SQL syntax for masking policies; CASE WHEN is the correct pattern.
questions.reference: Snowflake Security — Dynamic Data Masking Policy Functions

questions.stem: A data analyst notices that after restarting a virtual warehouse and running their first query, it is slower than subsequent identical queries. What BEST explains this behavior?

options.text: The Cloud Services layer needs to re-authenticate the user after each warehouse restart.
The first query warms up the local disk cache on the warehouse nodes; subsequent queries benefit from cached micro-partition data.
The query result cache is cleared when a warehouse restarts, forcing re-computation.
The optimizer generates a less efficient plan for the first query after a restart.

questions.explanation: B is correct because the local disk (SSD) cache on warehouse nodes is empty after a restart. The first query must read micro-partitions from remote object storage (S3/Azure/GCS), which is slower. Subsequent identical queries can read from the populated SSD cache, which is much faster. This is called cache warming. A is wrong because authentication is handled by the Cloud Services layer and is not affected by warehouse restarts. C is wrong because the query result cache is in the Cloud Services layer and is not cleared when a warehouse restarts. D is wrong because the optimizer uses the same plan — the difference is the data access path.
questions.reference: Snowflake Performance — Local Disk Cache Warming

questions.stem: A developer runs `EXPLAIN USING TABULAR SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id = 12345`. They see a TableScan node scanning all 10,000 micro-partitions. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?

options.text: The query uses SELECT * which disables partition pruning.
The customer_id column has poor clustering — its values are spread across all micro-partitions.
The EXPLAIN command itself prevents pruning from being evaluated.
The orders table has too few micro-partitions to benefit from pruning.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake prunes micro-partitions using column min/max metadata. If customer_id values are randomly distributed across all micro-partitions (i.e., the min/max range of every partition includes 12345), no partitions can be eliminated and a full scan occurs. A is wrong because SELECT * does not disable partition pruning — the WHERE clause drives pruning regardless of the SELECT list. C is wrong because EXPLAIN evaluates the query plan including pruning estimates. D is wrong because having 10,000 micro-partitions is a large table, not too few — and poor clustering on customer_id is the likely culprit.
questions.reference: Snowflake Performance — Partition Pruning, Clustering

questions.stem: A materialized view is defined on a base table in Snowflake. The base table receives frequent INSERT and DELETE operations. Which statement CORRECTLY describes how the materialized view is maintained?

options.text: The materialized view is rebuilt from scratch each time the base table changes.
Snowflake automatically and transparently maintains the materialized view using background compute, consuming credits.
The user must manually refresh the materialized view by calling ALTER MATERIALIZED VIEW ... REFRESH.
The materialized view becomes stale and is automatically dropped after 7 days without a manual refresh.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake automatically maintains materialized views in the background whenever the base table changes. This maintenance consumes compute credits and happens transparently without user intervention. The optimizer can then use the materialized view to serve queries. A is wrong because materialized views are incrementally maintained, not fully rebuilt from scratch. C is wrong because Snowflake manages automatic refresh — unlike some other systems, users do not need to manually refresh Snowflake materialized views. D is wrong because materialized views do not have a 7-day staleness limit or auto-drop behavior.
questions.reference: Snowflake Performance — Materialized Views Auto-Maintenance

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is investigating a query that runs for 45 minutes on a Large warehouse. The Query Profile shows that 95% of the time is spent in a HashJoin node. Which TWO actions could potentially improve performance? (Select TWO)

options.text: Increase the warehouse size to XL or larger to provide more memory for the hash join.
Enable the Search Optimization Service on the table.
Rewrite the query to filter earlier and reduce the number of rows reaching the join.
Create a network policy to restrict concurrent access during query execution.

questions.explanation: A and C are correct. Hash joins are memory-intensive — a larger warehouse provides more memory per node, reducing disk spilling and improving join performance. Filtering rows earlier (pushing predicates before the join) reduces the data volume the join must process, directly improving performance. B is wrong because the Search Optimization Service improves point-lookup queries, not join operations. D is wrong because network policies control connectivity and have no effect on query execution performance.
questions.reference: Snowflake Performance — Warehouse Sizing, Query Optimization

questions.stem: The `SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION` function returns `total_partition_count: 5000` and `total_constant_partition_count: 4800`. What does this indicate?

options.text: 4800 micro-partitions are completely prunable for any query on the clustering key.
4800 of the 5000 micro-partitions contain only a single distinct value of the clustering key, meaning the table is very well-clustered.
4800 micro-partitions have not been reclustered recently and need maintenance.
4800 micro-partitions exceed the recommended maximum size and should be split.

questions.explanation: B is correct because `total_constant_partition_count` represents the number of micro-partitions that contain only a single distinct value of the clustering key column. A high ratio (4800/5000 = 96%) indicates excellent clustering — queries filtering on the clustering key can eliminate nearly all micro-partitions quickly. A is wrong because constant partitions are well-clustered, not necessarily prunable for all queries — they indicate homogeneity within each partition. C is wrong because a high constant partition count indicates good clustering, not maintenance needs. D is wrong because this metric is not related to partition size.
questions.reference: Snowflake Performance — Clustering Information, Constant Partitions

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to load CSV files from their local machine into a Snowflake table. They first need to upload the files to a Snowflake stage. Which stage type allows files to be uploaded directly from a local machine using the PUT command?

options.text: External stage (S3)
Internal named stage
External stage (Azure Blob)
Table stage using COPY INTO directly

questions.explanation: B is correct because the PUT command can upload files from a local machine to an internal stage (user stage `@~`, table stage `@%table`, or internal named stage). Internal stages store files in Snowflake-managed storage. A is wrong because you cannot use the PUT command to upload to an external stage — files in external stages must be uploaded via the cloud provider's tools (AWS CLI, Azure Storage Explorer, etc.). C is wrong for the same reason as A. D is wrong because COPY INTO is used to load data from a stage into a table, not to upload files from a local machine.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Loading — PUT Command, Internal Stages

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to unload data from a Snowflake table to an S3 bucket. Which command accomplishes this?

options.text: `EXPORT TABLE orders TO @my_s3_stage;`
`COPY INTO @my_s3_stage FROM orders FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = CSV);`
`INSERT INTO @my_s3_stage SELECT * FROM orders;`
`UNLOAD orders INTO @my_s3_stage;`

questions.explanation: B is correct because `COPY INTO @stage FROM table` is the Snowflake command for data unloading. It exports data from a table or query to files in a named stage (including external stages pointing to S3). A is wrong because EXPORT TABLE is not a valid Snowflake command. C is wrong because INSERT INTO cannot write to a stage. D is wrong because UNLOAD is not a valid Snowflake command.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Loading — COPY INTO for Data Unloading

questions.stem: A team is loading 500 GB of gzipped CSV files using COPY INTO. Each file is approximately 2 GB compressed. What is the RECOMMENDED approach to maximize parallel loading throughput?

options.text: Load all files in a single COPY INTO statement and use ON_ERROR = CONTINUE to handle any errors.
Split the 2 GB files into multiple smaller files of 100–250 MB compressed before loading.
Use a larger warehouse (e.g., 4X-Large) with a single 2 GB file for maximum parallelism.
Convert files to JSON format before loading, as JSON enables better parallel processing in Snowflake.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake parallelizes loading by assigning individual files to different threads. Files that are too large cannot be split by a single COPY thread — the recommended file size is 100–250 MB compressed. Splitting the 2 GB files into ~20 smaller files each allows Snowflake to load them in parallel across warehouse nodes. A is wrong because having fewer, larger files limits parallelism regardless of ON_ERROR settings. C is wrong because a larger warehouse cannot parallelize within a single large file — the bottleneck is file count, not warehouse size. D is wrong because JSON does not inherently enable better parallelism than CSV — file size is the key factor.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Loading — File Size Recommendations for Parallel Loading

questions.stem: What is the purpose of the `SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA()` function in a Snowflake task?

options.text: It returns the number of rows in the stream waiting to be processed.
It returns TRUE if there is unconsumed change data in the stream, allowing a task to skip execution if the stream is empty.
It forces the stream to materialize all pending changes to a staging table.
It resets the stream offset to the beginning of Time Travel.

questions.explanation: B is correct because `SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA()` is commonly used as a WHEN condition in a task definition to conditionally skip execution when there is no new data in the stream. It returns TRUE when the stream has unconsumed rows and FALSE when empty. A is wrong because it returns a boolean, not a row count. C is wrong because it does not materialize or process data. D is wrong because it does not reset the stream offset.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Transformations — SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to build a task DAG where `task_b` only runs after `task_a` completes successfully. Which DDL statement creates this dependency?

options.text: `CREATE TASK task_b AFTER task_a SCHEDULE = '5 MINUTE' AS ...`
`CREATE TASK task_b AFTER task_a AS ...`
`CREATE TASK task_b DEPENDS ON task_a SCHEDULE = '5 MINUTE' AS ...`
`ALTER TASK task_b SET PREDECESSOR = 'task_a';`

questions.explanation: B is correct because the `AFTER` clause in `CREATE TASK` establishes a dependency — task_b will be triggered after task_a completes successfully. Child tasks in a DAG do not have their own SCHEDULE; the root task's schedule drives the entire DAG. A is wrong because a child task in a DAG cannot have its own SCHEDULE — the schedule is only on the root task. C is wrong because DEPENDS ON is not valid Snowflake syntax for task dependencies. D is wrong because SET PREDECESSOR is not a valid ALTER TASK option.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Transformations — Task DAGs, AFTER Clause

questions.stem: A data engineer writes a `MERGE INTO` statement to upsert data from a staging table into a target table. Which clause handles rows present in the staging table but NOT in the target table?

options.text: WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT
WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN DELETE
WHEN MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN INSERT

questions.explanation: B is correct because `WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT` handles rows in the source (staging) table that do not have a matching row in the target table — these are new rows that need to be inserted. A is wrong because WHEN MATCHED applies when a row exists in both source and target (for updates). C is wrong because WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE handles rows in the TARGET that have no match in the source, typically used for deletes. D is wrong because WHEN MATCHED BY SOURCE is not a valid Snowflake MERGE clause.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Transformations — MERGE INTO Statement

questions.stem: Which TWO statements correctly describe Snowflake Dynamic Tables? (Select TWO)

options.text: Dynamic tables can use FULL or INCREMENTAL refresh modes, with Snowflake selecting the method based on the query complexity.
Dynamic tables require a user-defined schedule in CRON format for refresh timing.
Dynamic tables can be chained — one dynamic table's refresh can depend on another dynamic table's lag.
Dynamic tables are only supported in the Business Critical edition and above.

questions.explanation: A and C are correct. Dynamic tables support both FULL refresh (recreating the entire result set) and INCREMENTAL refresh (processing only changed rows), with Snowflake selecting the appropriate method based on whether the query can be incrementally evaluated. Dynamic tables can also be chained — a downstream dynamic table can set `TARGET_LAG = DOWNSTREAM` to inherit its lag from the pipeline upstream. B is wrong because dynamic tables use `TARGET_LAG` (a staleness duration), not a user-defined CRON schedule — Snowflake determines when to refresh. D is wrong because dynamic tables are available in Enterprise edition and above, not only Business Critical.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Transformations — Dynamic Tables

questions.stem: A developer creates a JavaScript UDF in Snowflake. Which limitation applies specifically to JavaScript UDFs in Snowflake?

options.text: JavaScript UDFs cannot accept more than one argument.
JavaScript UDFs cannot be used in SELECT statements.
JavaScript UDFs cannot issue SQL queries (they cannot execute SQL inside the function body).
JavaScript UDFs can only return INTEGER or VARCHAR data types.

questions.explanation: C is correct because JavaScript UDFs in Snowflake are scalar functions that cannot execute SQL statements inside their function body — they can only process input arguments and return a value using JavaScript logic. If you need to execute SQL within a function, you must use a stored procedure instead. A is wrong because JavaScript UDFs can accept multiple arguments. B is wrong because JavaScript UDFs can be called in SELECT, WHERE, and other clauses. D is wrong because JavaScript UDFs can return a variety of Snowflake data types, not just INTEGER or VARCHAR.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Transformations — JavaScript UDF Limitations

questions.stem: A data engineer needs an auto-incrementing surrogate key when inserting rows into a table. Which Snowflake feature provides this capability?

options.text: A column with IDENTITY property (e.g., id INT IDENTITY(1,1))
A stream with METADATA$ROW_ID
A task that calls NEXTVAL on insert
A materialized view with ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY 1)

questions.explanation: A is correct because Snowflake supports the IDENTITY property on integer columns, which automatically generates sequential values for each inserted row. Sequences can also be used explicitly with `nextval`, but the IDENTITY column property is the most direct way to create an auto-incrementing surrogate key. B is wrong because METADATA$ROW_ID is an internal Snowflake identifier in streams, not a user-accessible auto-increment key. C is wrong because a task is not the mechanism for generating surrogate keys during inserts. D is wrong because ROW_NUMBER() in a materialized view does not generate persistent surrogate keys for new rows.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Transformations — Sequences and IDENTITY

questions.stem: A data analyst accidentally deletes all rows from the `transactions` table at 10:00 AM. The table has a Time Travel retention of 1 day. Which query returns the table data as it existed at 9:55 AM?

options.text: `SELECT * FROM transactions BEFORE(TIMESTAMP => '2024-01-15 09:55:00'::TIMESTAMP_TZ);`
`SELECT * FROM transactions AT(OFFSET => -300);`
`SELECT * FROM transactions SNAPSHOT AT('09:55:00');`
`SELECT * FROM transactions HISTORY WHERE timestamp = '09:55:00';`

questions.explanation: B is correct because `AT(OFFSET => -300)` queries the table as it existed 300 seconds (5 minutes) before the current time. If the query is run shortly after 10:00 AM, an offset of -300 seconds points to approximately 9:55 AM. Note: AT(TIMESTAMP => ...) is also valid, making A partially correct in concept, but B uses the correct OFFSET syntax shown in the options. A uses BEFORE instead of AT — BEFORE returns data as of just before a statement or timestamp, not AT a timestamp; however, the syntax shown (`BEFORE(TIMESTAMP => ...)`) would return data just before 9:55 AM, which is effectively the same. For this question, B demonstrates the canonical OFFSET approach. C is wrong because SNAPSHOT AT is not valid Snowflake Time Travel syntax. D is wrong because no HISTORY clause exists in Snowflake SQL.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Protection — Time Travel AT Clause

questions.stem: A Snowflake data provider creates a SHARE and grants SELECT on a secure view to a consumer account. The consumer creates a database from the share. Which statement is TRUE about the shared data?

options.text: The consumer can see the DDL definition of the secure view since they have SELECT on it.
Data is copied from the provider's account to the consumer's account when the shared database is created.
The consumer accesses the provider's data in real time with no data movement; the provider's storage is read directly.
The consumer can write data back to the shared tables if they have INSERT privileges.

questions.explanation: C is correct because Snowflake Data Sharing provides zero-copy, live access to data. The consumer reads the provider's data directly from the provider's storage with no data movement or replication. This means changes in the provider's data are immediately visible to the consumer. A is wrong because secure views specifically hide the DDL from non-owners, including consumers. B is wrong because data sharing explicitly does NOT copy data — the consumer reads the provider's live data. D is wrong because shared objects are read-only from the consumer's perspective; consumers cannot write back to shared tables.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Protection — Data Sharing, Zero-Copy
